colon adenocarcinoma: The image shows infiltrative, irregularly shaped glands invading the submucosa.
A key feature is the presence of cribriform structures, where glands appear fused with back-to-back lumens.
This photo displays malignant glands with a loss of normal crypt architecture and cellular polarity.
Visible are tumor cells with pleomorphic, hyperchromatic nuclei and a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio.
The image reveals areas of 'dirty necrosis', characterized by necrotic debris within the glandular lumens.
This is an image showing a desmoplastic stromal reaction, an abundance of fibrous tissue surrounding the invading glands.
The malignant cells demonstrate prominent nucleoli and irregular nuclear membranes.
This photo shows a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma with tumor cells arranged in solid sheets or single files.
In the image, perineural invasion is visible, with cancer cells surrounding a nerve bundle.
A well-differentiated adenocarcinoma is seen, with glands that still retain some resemblance to normal colonic crypts but are crowded.
This image displays lymphovascular invasion, where tumor cell clusters are present within a lymphatic or blood vessel.
The photo shows a signet-ring cell carcinoma variant, with cells containing large cytoplasmic mucin vacuoles that displace the nucleus to the periphery.
Tumor budding is evident at the invasive front, characterized by isolated single cells or small clusters of tumor cells.
This is an image of mucinous adenocarcinoma, where more than 50% of the tumor volume is composed of extracellular mucin pools.
The glands show architectural complexity, including branching and papillary formations not seen in normal tissue.
colon benign tissue: The image displays well-formed, straight, tubular crypts arranged parallel to each other.
A key feature is the preservation of the normal crypt architecture, with crypts extending down to the muscularis mucosae.
This photo shows a uniform population of absorptive cells and goblet cells lining the crypts.
Visible are uniform, round to oval nuclei that are basally located within the epithelial cells.
The image reveals an abundance of mucin-producing goblet cells, appearing as clear, vacuolated spaces.
This is an image of normal colonic mucosa with a low nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio in the epithelial cells.
The lamina propria contains a normal, mild inflammatory infiltrate, primarily lymphocytes and plasma cells.
This photo shows an intact muscularis mucosae, a thin layer of muscle separating the mucosa from the submucosa.
In the image, there is no evidence of nuclear pleomorphism, hyperchromasia, or prominent nucleoli.
The crypts are separated by a normal amount of lamina propria, without any back-to-back gland formation.
This image shows the smooth mucosal surface of the colon without any polyps, ulcers, or masses.
The epithelial cells show normal maturation from the base of the crypt to the surface.
This is a photo of benign colonic tissue, characterized by the complete absence of invasive glands or desmoplasia.
The cellularity of the lamina propria is within normal limits, without a dense inflammatory infiltrate.
The image confirms the absence of architectural distortion, such as the branching or serrated patterns seen in polyps.
lung adenocarcinoma: The image displays a lepidic growth pattern, with tumor cells proliferating along the surface of intact alveolar walls.
This photo shows an acinar pattern, characterized by the formation of well-defined glands and tubular structures.
A key feature is a papillary growth pattern, where tumor cells grow on fibrovascular cores.
Visible in the image is a micropapillary pattern, consisting of small, tuft-like clusters of tumor cells without fibrovascular cores.
The photo shows a solid growth pattern with sheets of tumor cells lacking any clear glandular differentiation.
This is an image of mucinous adenocarcinoma, with goblet cells or columnar cells filled with abundant intracytoplasmic mucin.
The tumor cells exhibit nuclear atypia, including enlargement, irregular contours, and vesicular chromatin.
This image reveals psammoma bodies, which are laminated microscopic calcifications, within the tumor.
The photo demonstrates stromal invasion, where malignant glands have breached the basement membrane and infiltrated the lung parenchyma.
Tumor cells are positive for TTF-1 (Thyroid Transcription Factor 1) on immunohistochemical staining, a key diagnostic marker.
This is an image of adenocarcinoma in situ, where the malignant cells are confined to the alveolar lining without invasion.
The image shows lymphatic invasion, with tumor emboli present in the lymphatic spaces.
A prominent desmoplastic stromal response is visible, indicating an invasive component.
The photo displays pleural invasion, where the tumor has extended to involve the visceral pleura.
The tumor cells have moderate amounts of cytoplasm, which can be eosinophilic or vacuolated.
lung benign tissue: The image displays preserved alveolar architecture with thin, delicate alveolar walls.
This photo shows clear, open alveolar spaces, essential for normal gas exchange.
A key feature is the presence of type I and type II pneumocytes lining the alveoli without any cytological atypia.
Visible in the image are normal bronchioles lined by ciliated columnar epithelium.
The photo shows normal pulmonary arteries and veins with no evidence of tumor emboli.
This is an image of lung parenchyma with a minimal number of alveolar macrophages in the airspaces.
The interstitial space is thin and contains only a few capillaries and minimal connective tissue.
This photo shows a segment of a bronchus with its characteristic hyaline cartilage plates and seromucous glands.
In the image, there is a complete absence of inflammation, fibrosis, or cellular infiltration in the interstitium.
The alveolar septa are intact and not thickened or lined by atypical cells.
This image shows the delicate, lace-like appearance of a healthy lung parenchyma at low magnification.
The lining of the bronchioles is uniform, and the underlying smooth muscle layer is unremarkable.
This is a photo of benign lung tissue, characterized by the absence of any nodules, masses, or infiltrates.
The pleura, if visible, is a thin, delicate membrane without any thickening or tumor deposits.
The overall cellularity is low, and there is no evidence of nuclear pleomorphism or mitotic activity.
lung squamous cell carcinoma: The image shows nests of malignant squamous cells with distinct intercellular bridges.
A key feature is the presence of keratinization, often forming characteristic 'keratin pearls'.
This photo displays tumor cells with abundant, dense, eosinophilic (pink) cytoplasm.
Visible are large, pleomorphic nuclei with hyperchromasia and irregular contours.
The image reveals areas of central necrosis within the large tumor nests, a common feature.
This is an image of an invasive carcinoma, with tumor nests infiltrating the surrounding lung stroma.
The tumor often shows a clear connection to the bronchial epithelium from which it arises.
This photo shows a well-differentiated tumor with prominent keratinization and obvious intercellular bridges.
In a poorly differentiated variant, keratinization may be subtle or absent, identifiable only by single dyskeratotic cells.
The tumor cells are arranged in solid sheets and nests with pushing, infiltrative borders.
This image may show areas of cavitation, resulting from extensive central tumor necrosis.
The mitotic activity is often brisk, with atypical mitotic figures visible.
Immunohistochemical staining would show strong positivity for squamous markers like p63 and CK5/6.
The tumor is characterized by its solid, firm, and often white-tan appearance on gross examination.
This is a photo of a central lung mass that is histologically confirmed as squamous cell carcinoma, often causing bronchial obstruction.
